_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against Endocrine Society 2015 Cushing's Guidelines, NICE NG24 Adrenal Insufficiency, UpToDate, and peer-reviewed literature.

h1Title: Cushing's Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Cushing's syndrome is a clinical disorder resulting from prolonged, excessive exposure of tissues to glucocorticoids — most commonly cortisol — producing a characteristic constellation of metabolic, cardiovascular, neuropsychiatric, and cosmetic manifestations that collectively represent one of the most recognizable syndromes in medicine. The condition is caused either by exogenous glucocorticoid administration (iatrogenic, the most common cause), a pituitary adenoma secreting adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) (Cushing's disease, the most common cause of endogenous disease), an ectopic ACTH-secreting tumor, or a primary adrenal tumor secreting cortisol. Clinical presentation includes central obesity, moon facies, purple striae, proximal muscle weakness, hypertension, and glucose intolerance. Diagnosis requires confirming elevated cortisol followed by identifying the source; treatment involves surgical resection of the tumor with adjunctive radiotherapy or medical therapy when surgery is insufficient or not feasible.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence of endogenous Cushing's syndrome is approximately 1-2 per 100,000 people per year; pituitary ACTH-secreting adenomas (Cushing's disease) account for approximately 70% of endogenous cases; ectopic ACTH tumors account for 10-15%; adrenal tumors 10-15%
keyStats.demographics: Cushing's disease (pituitary) occurs 3-5 times more frequently in women than men; ectopic ACTH tumors occur equally in men and women; adrenal Cushing's has no strong gender preference
keyStats.avgAge: Cushing's disease typically presents between ages 30-50; ectopic ACTH tumors (small cell lung cancer) typically present in older adults (60-70 years); adrenal tumors peak in the 4th-5th decade
keyStats.globalCases: Iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome (from prolonged corticosteroid therapy) is far more common than endogenous disease and represents the majority of clinical Cushing's presentations globally

definition: Cushing's syndrome (ICD-10: E24.9 for Cushing's syndrome, unspecified; E24.0 for pituitary-dependent Cushing's disease; E24.1 for Nelson syndrome; E24.2 for drug-induced Cushing's syndrome; E24.3 for ectopic ACTH syndrome; E24.4 for alcohol-induced pseudo-Cushing's; E24.8 for other Cushing's syndrome; E24.9 for Cushing's syndrome; M04.1 for Cushingoid syndrome) is the clinical syndrome resulting from sustained hypercortisolism. The biochemical hallmark is loss of normal cortisol circadian rhythm and elevated 24-hour urinary free cortisol. Endogenous Cushing's is classified as ACTH-dependent (ACTH secreted by a pituitary adenoma or ectopic tumor, stimulating the adrenal glands to overproduce cortisol) or ACTH-independent (primary adrenal pathology or exogenous glucocorticoids). Cushing's disease specifically refers to ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma. Diagnosis involves demonstrating hypercortisolism (late-night salivary cortisol, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, or 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test), then establishing the source (pituitary vs ectopic vs adrenal) using high-dose dexamethasone suppression testing, ACTH measurement, and pituitary or other tumor imaging. Managed by endocrinologists, neurosurgeons, and in some cases oncologists.

typesClassification.description: The most common form globally, caused by prolonged administration of exogenous glucocorticoids (prednisone, dexamethasone, hydrocortisone) for autoimmune, inflammatory, or malignant conditions. The degree of hypercortisolism depends on dose and duration. Side effects are dose-dependent. Adrenal suppression occurs because the exogenous steroids suppress the HPA axis. Tapering must be gradual to allow adrenal recovery.

The most common cause of endogenous Cushing's syndrome (~70% of cases). A benign pituitary adenoma secretes ACTH independently, driving bilateral adrenal hyperplasia and cortisol overproduction. Typically microadenomas (<1 cm), presenting with the full spectrum of Cushing's features. Diagnosis: elevated ACTH, positive high-dose dexamethasone suppression (>50% cortisol suppression), pituitary MRI showing adenoma.

A non-pituitary tumor secretes ACTH (or less commonly CRH), causing bilateral adrenal hyperplasia and hypercortisolism. Most commonly small cell lung carcinoma, bronchial carcinoid, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, and medullary thyroid carcinoma. Often presents with rapid-onset, severe hypokalemia and hyperpigmentation from very high ACTH levels.

Primary adrenal tumors (adenomas, carcinomas, or rarely bilateral adrenal hyperplasia) secrete cortisol autonomously, suppressing ACTH. Causes include cortisol-secreting adrenal adenoma (most common adrenal cause), adrenal carcinoma (often virilizing, rapidly progressive), and primary bilateral macronodular adrenal hyperplasia (rare). Diagnosis: elevated cortisol, suppressed ACTH, adrenal imaging showing unilateral or bilateral masses.

Conditions with hypercortisolism that clinically mimic Cushing's but are caused by HPA axis activation rather than autonomous cortisol production: severe depression, alcohol use disorder (alcohol-induced pseudo-Cushing's), glucocorticoid resistance, and PCOS. These normalize cortisol after treatment of the underlying condition. Important to distinguish from true Cushing's before invasive testing.

primarySymptoms: Central obesity and weight gain — fat redistribution to the trunk and abdomen (visceral adiposity) with preserved or thin extremities, producing a 'lemon on stick' appearance. Contrasts with generalized obesity from simple weight gain.
Moon facies — rounded, plethoric face with full cheeks and fat deposition in the preauricular area, giving a characteristic rounded appearance that develops over months.
Dorsocervical and supraclavicular fat pads — 'buffalo hump' (fat deposition at the back of the neck and upper back) and supraclavicular fat pads are highly specific for Cushing's when present.

Purple striae — wide (>1 cm), violaceous (purple-red) atrophic striae on the abdomen, flanks, breasts, thighs, and upper arms. Unlike normal white striae (from growth or weight change), purple striae in Cushing's reflect dermal protein catabolism from cortisol excess.

Proximal muscle weakness — difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, or raising arms above the head; reflects cortisol-induced myopathy affecting type II muscle fibers. Distinct from generalized weakness from deconditioning.
Hypertension — cortisol acts as a mineralocorticoid at high concentrations, causing sodium retention, volume expansion, and elevated blood pressure. Present in approximately 80% of patients.

Glucose intolerance and diabetes — cortisol increases hepatic gluconeogenesis, reduces glucose uptake in muscle and fat, and promotes visceral adiposity; approximately 20-40% of Cushing's patients have diabetes; additional 20-30% have prediabetes.
Skin fragility and easy bruising — thin skin, easy bruising from minor trauma, poor wound healing, and facial plethora from cortisol-induced skin protein catabolism.
Hirsutism and acne — excess androgens from adrenal stimulation cause facial and body hair growth and acne in some patients.
Osteoporosis and fractures — cortisol suppresses osteoblast activity and calcium absorption, reducing bone density; vertebral compression fractures occur in 20-40% of patients.
earlyWarningSigns: Rapid weight gain with central distribution in a previously healthy adult
New-onset hypertension or diabetes in a young or middle-aged adult
Easy bruising or purple striae on the abdomen or flanks in someone not recently pregnant or rapidly weight fluctuating
Proximal muscle weakness — difficulty with activities that were previously easy
Facial rounding ('moon facies') or fat pad development at the back of the neck ('buffalo hump')
Irregular periods or loss of libido in women — cortisol suppresses the HPA axis, reducing GnRH pulsatility
Cognitive and mood changes — new anxiety, depression, or cognitive impairment without other explanation
Unexplained hypokalemia — particularly with hypertension, suggest ectopic ACTH with severe potassium wasting
emergencySigns: Acute psychosis or severe psychiatric decompensation — cortisol excess can cause acute psychosis with paranoid delusions and hallucinations, requiring urgent endocrinology and psychiatry evaluation
Hypertensive crisis — severe, resistant hypertension from cortisol excess or from an underlying pheochromocytoma co-secreting ACTH in MEN2 syndrome
Severe hypokalemia — ectopic ACTH tumors can cause life-threatening hypokalemia (<2.5 mEq/L) with cardiac arrhythmias and muscle paralysis
Vertebral compression fracture — osteoporosis from Cushing's can cause spontaneous or minimally traumatic vertebral fractures with severe back pain
Disseminated intravascular coagulation or severe infection — severe hypercortisolism causes immunosuppression and coagulopathy; opportunistic infections can develop rapidly

Acute adrenal insufficiency during treatment tapering — patients on chronic glucocorticoids (even tapered doses) can develop adrenal crisis if dose is reduced too rapidly or if physiologic stress occurs without dose increase

causes.description: Prolonged use of oral, parenteral, or high-dose inhaled corticosteroids is the most common cause of Cushing's syndrome worldwide. Prednisone, dexamethasone, and hydrocortisone in supraphysiological doses cause identical features to endogenous Cushing's. Risk is dose- and duration-dependent. Adrenal suppression complicates tapering.

A benign pituitary tumor secretes ACTH, driving bilateral adrenal hyperplasia and cortisol overproduction. Accounts for ~70% of endogenous Cushing's syndrome. Most are microadenomas (<1 cm). Diagnosis: elevated ACTH, cortisol suppression with high-dose dexamethasone, pituitary MRI positive in 50-70% (small adenomas may be missed). Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling (BIPSS) confirms pituitary source.

Non-pituitary tumors (small cell lung carcinoma, bronchial/mediastinal carcinoid tumors, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma) secrete ACTH or CRH. Presents with rapid-onset, severe hypercortisolism, profound hypokalemia, and hypertension. Diagnosis: elevated ACTH, no cortisol suppression with high-dose dexamethasone, negative pituitary MRI, abnormal chest/abdominal imaging.

Cortisol-secreting adrenal adenomas or carcinomas cause ACTH-independent Cushing's. Present with a unilateral adrenal mass, elevated cortisol, and suppressed ACTH. Adrenal carcinomas may co-secrete androgens (causing virilization) and present with a palpable abdominal mass and rapid progression.

Rare causes: primary bilateral macronodular adrenal hyperplasia (PBMAH, ACTH-independent) and primary pigmented nodular adrenal dysplasia (PPNAD, often in Carney complex). Present with bilateral adrenal enlargement, suppressed ACTH, and hypercortisolism.

riskFactors.description: Prolonged use of oral, injectable, or high-dose inhaled corticosteroids for autoimmune conditions (rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, inflammatory bowel disease), asthma, or organ transplantation is the leading risk factor for iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome.
MEN1 syndrome (parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreatic tumors) includes pituitary adenomas, some of which secrete ACTH. Sporadic pituitary adenomas are common but most do not cause Cushing's disease.

riskFactors.factor: Neuroendocrine tumors (small cell lung cancer, carcinoid)
riskFactors.description: Small cell lung carcinoma, bronchial carcinoid tumors, and other neuroendocrine tumors can ectopically secrete ACTH. Smoking is the major risk factor for small cell lung carcinoma.

Adrenal adenomas and carcinomas occur sporadically or as part of genetic syndromes: MEN2 (medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, parathyroid hyperplasia), familial paraganglioma syndromes, and Li-Fraumeni syndrome. Adrenal incidentalomas occasionally secrete cortisol ('subclinical Cushing's').
Severe major depression and some psychiatric conditions can cause hypercortisolism that mimics Cushing's (pseudo-Cushing's). This must be excluded before invasive testing for true Cushing's.
Chronic alcohol use can cause alcohol-induced pseudo-Cushing's, with clinical features identical to Cushing's but resolving with alcohol cessation. Mechanism involves CRH and ACTH axis activation.

affectedDemographics: Endogenous Cushing's syndrome incidence is 1-2 per 100,000 people per year; iatrogenic Cushing's is far more common given the widespread use of chronic corticosteroid therapy
Cushing's disease (pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma) affects women 3-5 times more frequently than men, suggesting estrogen may play a role in tumor development
Peak incidence of Cushing's disease is between ages 30-50; ectopic ACTH tumors (small cell lung cancer) peak in older adults (60-70 years)
Adrenal Cushing's has no strong gender preference and presents in the 4th-5th decade
Pseudo-Cushing's from depression and alcohol is common and must be distinguished from true Cushing's — up to 50% of patients with Cushing's have comorbid depression
Nelson syndrome (rapidly enlarging pituitary tumor after bilateral adrenalectomy) occurs in 10-25% of Cushing's disease patients who undergo bilateral adrenalectomy
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis proceeds in three steps: (1) Confirm hypercortisolism — using one of: late-night salivary cortisol (loss of circadian rhythm), 24-hour urinary free cortisol (elevated), or 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (failure to suppress cortisol below 1.8 mcg/dL). Two abnormal tests confirm hypercortisolism. (2) Establish ACTH-dependence — measure serum ACTH. ACTH >20 pg/mL = ACTH-dependent (pituitary or ectopic); ACTH <10 pg/mL = ACTH-independent (adrenal or exogenous). (3) Determine the source — for ACTH-dependent: high-dose dexamethasone suppression test (8 mg overnight or 2-day low-dose); pituitary MRI; if MRI negative or equivocal, bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling (BIPSS) is the gold standard. For ACTH-independent: adrenal CT/MRI to identify unilateral or bilateral adrenal pathology. Note: common medications (phenytoin, rifampin, carbamazepine) accelerate dexamethasone metabolism, causing false-positive suppression test.

diagnosticTests.test: Late-night salivary cortisol (2 samples, 2 days apart)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive initial screening test. Loss of normal circadian cortisol rhythm — midnight cortisol is elevated (normally near zero). Ideally collected at 11 PM using a specialized swab device. No false positives from stress if collected properly.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Saliva collection using a specialized swab at 11 PM on 2 separate nights. Kit mailed to the patient; samples sent to lab. Results in days. Avoid eating, brushing teeth, or using corticosteroid creams near the collection time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures cortisol output over 24 hours; elevated in Cushing's syndrome. Specific but less sensitive than late-night salivary cortisol — requires proper collection (complete 24-hour collection without missing any urine). Artificially elevated in high urine volume.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Complete 24-hour urine collection on 2 separate days (often including a urine creatinine to verify collection adequacy). Keep collection refrigerated. Results in days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Dexamethasone suppresses cortisol in normal individuals (negative feedback). In Cushing's, cortisol fails to suppress below 1.8 mcg/dL (50 nmol/L). Simple and specific when positive. False positives occur with medications that accelerate dexamethasone metabolism (phenytoin, rifampin, carbamazepine, estrogens).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Take 1 mg dexamethasone at 11 PM; blood draw at 8 AM next morning for cortisol. Must be performed at a time when patient is not on interacting medications. Can be done as outpatient.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes ACTH-dependent (Cushing's disease, ectopic ACTH) from ACTH-independent (adrenal tumor, exogenous steroids). Critical second step after confirming hypercortisolism. Must be measured before any glucocorticoid administration that would suppress ACTH.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw. Must be done in the morning when ACTH is highest and most distinguishable. Results in days. If elevated, proceed to source localization.

diagnosticTests.test: High-dose dexamethasone suppression test (8 mg overnight or 2-day)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates pituitary ACTH source (where high-dose dexamethasone suppresses cortisol >50%) from ectopic source (where it does not suppress). Helpful but not definitive — some ectopic tumors suppress, and some pituitary tumors do not.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 8 mg dexamethasone at 11 PM with morning cortisol; or 0.5 mg q6h for 48 hours with 24-hour urine collections. Used in conjunction with ACTH level and imaging.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pituitary adenoma in ACTH-dependent disease. Microadenomas (<6 mm) may not be visible — a negative MRI does not rule out Cushing's disease. When positive with concordant biochemistry, surgery can proceed without BIPSS.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI of sella turcica with gadolinium contrast. Takes 30-45 minutes. Requires IV contrast. Detects adenomas >6 mm with ~70% sensitivity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for confirming pituitary ACTH source when MRI is negative or discordant with biochemistry. Catheters are placed in the petrosal sinuses draining the pituitary; ACTH is measured before and after CRH stimulation from both petrosal sinuses vs peripheral blood. A central-to-peripheral ACTH ratio >2 (or >3 after CRH) confirms pituitary source.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Invasive procedure performed by interventional radiology. Femoral venous access; catheters advanced to petrosal sinuses. CRH administered as IV bolus. Requires specialist center with experienced interventional radiology team.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of Cushing's syndrome is surgical when possible — resection of the causative tumor. Transsphenoidal surgery (TSS) by an experienced pituitary neurosurgeon is the first-line treatment for Cushing's disease (pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma), with initial remission rates of 65-90% in experienced hands. Bilateral adrenalectomy is an effective second-line option for persistent Cushing's disease after failed pituitary surgery. For ectopic ACTH syndrome, surgical resection of the tumor (or curative resection of a carcinoid) is curative; if the tumor is unresectable (small cell lung cancer), bilateral adrenalectomy or medical therapy (ketoconazole, metyrapone, mifepristone, etomidate) is used to control hypercortisolism. For adrenal Cushing's, unilateral adrenalectomy is curative. Postoperative glucocorticoid replacement is required until the HPA axis recovers (6-18 months). Cushing's disease that persists or recurs after surgery may be treated with repeat TSS, pituitary radiotherapy, radiosurgery (Gamma Knife), or bilateral adrenalectomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Transsphenoidal surgery (TSS) for Cushing's disease
medicalTreatments.description: Endoscopic or microscopic surgical resection of the pituitary adenoma via the transsphenoidal approach (through the nose/sphenoid sinus). The first-line treatment for confirmed Cushing's disease. Requires a highly experienced pituitary neurosurgeon. Remission requires complete tumor resection; post-operatively, glucocorticoids are given until HPA axis recovers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Initial remission rates 65-90% in experienced centers; recurrence rates 10-30% over 5-10 years. Mortality and morbidity significantly reduced with high-volume surgeons. Biochemical confirmation (post-op cortisol <2 mcg/dL) predicts durable remission.

medicalTreatments.name: Ketoconazole 400-1200 mg/day (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: An antifungal agent that inhibits multiple steroidogenic enzymes (CYP17, CYP11A1, CYP11B1), reducing cortisol synthesis. First-line medical therapy for Cushing's syndrome. Requires liver function monitoring (hepatotoxicity risk). GI side effects common. Effective for all Cushing's types but often used as adjunct or pre-surgical bridge.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls hypercortisolism in 50-70% of patients. Often used as monotherapy or in combination with metyrapone. Typically requires dose titration. Commonly used as bridging therapy before or after surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Metyrapone 750-6000 mg/day (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: Blocks 11-beta-hydroxylase (CYP11B1), the final enzyme step of cortisol synthesis. Rapidly reduces cortisol levels. Used for hypercortisolism control in Cushing's disease, ectopic ACTH syndrome, and pre-operatively. Side effects include GI upset, hypertension, hypokalemia, and hirsutism from elevated androgen precursors.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in 50-60% of patients; often used in combination with ketoconazole for resistant cases. Particularly useful in ectopic ACTH syndrome where rapid control of severe hypercortisolism is needed.

medicalTreatments.name: Mifepristone (RU-486) 300-1200 mg/day (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: A glucocorticoid receptor antagonist that blocks cortisol action at the receptor without reducing cortisol production. FDA-approved for Cushing's syndrome (hypertension and glucose intolerance in patients with endogenous hypercortisolism). Particularly useful when hyperglycemia is prominent. Not used for diagnostic testing — blocks all cortisol activity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in reducing metabolic complications (hypertension, glucose intolerance) and providing symptomatic relief. Does not reduce cortisol levels — hypokalemia and hypertension from mineralocorticoid effects of cortisol may persist.

medicalTreatments.name: Pasireotide (subcutaneous) 0.3-1.8 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: A somatostatin receptor ligand (SRL) that binds to somatostatin receptor subtype 5 (SSTR5), which is highly expressed in corticotroph pituitary tumors. Reduces ACTH and cortisol secretion. Administered as twice-daily subcutaneous injection. Can cause hyperglycemia (by inhibiting insulin secretion).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Demonstrated cortisol normalization in ~40% of Cushing's disease patients in phase 3 trials. Particularly useful for patients with persistent disease after surgery who are not candidates for repeat surgery or radiotherapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral adrenalectomy for refractory Cushing's disease
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of both adrenal glands. A definitive cure for hypercortisolism but results in permanent adrenal insufficiency requiring lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement. Reserved for persistent or recurrent Cushing's disease after failed pituitary surgery, or in severe ectopic ACTH syndrome where the tumor is unresectable.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive cure of hypercortisolism. Requires lifelong adrenal hormone replacement. Nelson syndrome (enlargement of residual pituitary tumor with high ACTH and pigmentation) occurs in 10-25% of Cushing's disease patients post-adrenalectomy.

surgicalOptions.name: Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife) for pituitary adenoma
surgicalOptions.description: Focused radiation delivered to the pituitary adenoma, causing tumor cell death and reducing ACTH secretion over months to years. Used for residual or recurrent Cushing's disease after failed surgery, or for patients with contraindications to repeat surgery. Takes 6-24 months to achieve cortisol normalization.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Biochemical remission in 60-80% of patients over 2-5 years. Hypopituitarism develops in 30-50% over long-term follow-up. Less invasive than repeat transsphenoidal surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Medical adrenalectomy (bilateral) for ectopic ACTH syndrome
alternativeTreatments.description: When ectopic ACTH tumors are unresectable (e.g., small cell lung cancer), medical adrenalectomy with ketoconazole, metyrapone, or mitotane achieves biochemical control of hypercortisolism. Bilateral adrenalectomy (surgical adrenalectomy) is an alternative for severe refractory cases.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Medical adrenalectomy controls hypercortisolism in 70-80% of patients with ectopic ACTH. Bilateral adrenalectomy is definitive but requires lifelong adrenal replacement.

alternativeTreatments.name: Etomidate infusion (IV) for acute severe hypercortisolism
alternativeTreatments.description: An IV anesthetic agent that rapidly inhibits 11-beta-hydroxylase. Used in acute settings (thyroid storm analog for Cushing's) where severe hypercortisolism requires urgent control (infections, psychosis, severe hyperglycemia). Requires ICU monitoring.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid cortisol reduction within hours. Used short-term for acute control before definitive or long-term medical therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist is essential for suspected Cushing's — diagnosis is complex and involves multiple steps that must be interpreted correctly. Neurosurgery consultation is needed for confirmed pituitary disease (transsphenoidal surgery). For ectopic ACTH tumors, oncology and thoracic surgery are involved. Adrenal surgery (for adrenal adenoma or carcinoma) requires a surgeon experienced in adrenal surgery. Post-treatment, endocrinology is needed for adrenal axis monitoring and glucocorticoid replacement management.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an endocrinologist with specific experience in Cushing's syndrome and adrenal-pituitary disease — this is a rare condition requiring specialized knowledge. For pituitary surgery, seek a high-volume pituitary neurosurgeon (>50 TSS/year). For adrenal surgery, seek a surgeon experienced in adrenalectomy. Academic pituitary centers typically have the most comprehensive multidisciplinary teams (endocrinology, neurosurgery, radiation oncology, radiology). Ask about their approach to diagnosis, their surgical remission rates, and their post-operative monitoring protocol.
preventionStrategies: Use the lowest effective corticosteroid dose for the shortest duration when clinically necessary — intermittent courses minimize Cushing's risk

For patients requiring long-term corticosteroids: use alternate-day dosing where possible; monitor for early Cushing's features; consider non-steroidal alternatives (e.g., biologics for autoimmune disease)
Smoking cessation — reduces risk of small cell lung carcinoma, the most common ectopic ACTH source
Regular monitoring of patients on chronic glucocorticoid therapy for signs of hypercortisolism
Consider pituitary incidentaloma surveillance in asymptomatic patients — early detection of ACTH-secreting adenomas before they cause clinically apparent Cushing's
lifestyleModifications: Blood pressure control — hypertension from Cushing's requires monitoring and treatment; ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred; thiazides and beta-blockers also appropriate

Glucose management — hyperglycemia from Cushing's requires monitoring; diet, exercise, and glucose-lowering medications as needed; glucocorticoid-induced diabetes often improves after Cushing's treatment
Bone health — osteoporosis from Cushing's requires calcium and vitamin D supplementation, weight-bearing exercise, and bisphosphonate therapy if indicated; DEXA scan at diagnosis
Mental health support — depression, anxiety, and cognitive impairment are common in Cushing's; psychiatric evaluation and treatment may be needed before and after definitive therapy

recommended: High-protein diet — cortisol excess causes muscle catabolism; adequate protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) helps preserve lean body mass and supports bone health
Calcium and vitamin D — 1200-1500 mg calcium and 800-1000 IU vitamin D daily to combat osteoporosis from cortisol excess
Potassium-rich foods — hypokalemia is common, especially with ectopic ACTH; bananas, oranges, potatoes, spinach, beans
Low-sodium diet — excess cortisol causes mineralocorticoid effects (sodium retention, hypertension); limiting sodium helps manage blood pressure
Small, frequent meals — cortisol excess increases appetite; structured eating prevents excessive caloric intake
avoid: Excess sodium — Cushing's causes sodium retention and hypertension; high-sodium foods (processed foods, cured meats, canned soups) worsen hypertension
Excessive calories — Cushing's promotes central obesity and glucose intolerance; avoid overconsumption even as appetite increases
Alcohol — alcohol can worsen pseudo-Cushing's in susceptible individuals; avoid to maintain diagnostic clarity and reduce additional HPA axis activation

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is beneficial for Cushing's patients in moderation, particularly after hypercortisolism is controlled. Aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week) improves cardiovascular risk and supports weight management. Resistance training is important for counteracting the muscle-wasting effects of cortisol. However, during active, uncontrolled Cushing's, osteoporosis significantly increases fracture risk — high-impact or contact exercise should be avoided until bone density is assessed and hypercortisolism is controlled. Walking, swimming, and light resistance exercise are appropriate during active disease.
dailyManagement: If on ketoconazole: take with food; monitor liver function tests every 2-3 months; report any nausea, vomiting, or yellowing of skin
If on metyrapone: monitor blood pressure and potassium; report weakness (possible hypokalemia) or excessive edema
If on mifepristone: monitor for hypokalemia; blood glucose monitoring for hyperglycemia management
Post-surgery: glucocorticoid replacement is essential until HPA axis recovers; do not stop or skip replacement doses without consulting your endocrinologist
Medical alert identification — a bracelet noting 'adrenal insufficiency' and 'requires hydrocortisone in stress' is essential for anyone on glucocorticoid replacement
Stress dosing: if on glucocorticoid replacement, any fever, illness, or injury requires increased glucocorticoid doses (typically double or triple) — know your sick day rules
prognosis: With successful treatment (tumor removal with biochemical remission), the prognosis for Cushing's syndrome is excellent — most features (hypertension, diabetes, proximal weakness, depression) improve significantly over months to years. However, some features are irreversible — osteoporosis and vertebral fractures persist, and skin striae and bruising tendency improve but may not fully resolve. The most serious long-term complication of untreated or persistently active Cushing's is cardiovascular disease (heart attack, stroke), which accounts for most excess mortality. With biochemical remission, the excess mortality normalizes toward the general population. Nelson syndrome (post-adrenalectomy pituitary tumor enlargement) requires ongoing MRI surveillance and can be treated with radiotherapy or repeat surgery.
recoveryTimeline: After successful transsphenoidal surgery for Cushing's disease: immediate postoperative period requires glucocorticoid replacement (IV hydrocortisone tapering to oral); biochemical remission assessed within 2-4 weeks (cortisol should be very low); HPA axis recovery occurs over 6-18 months — glucocorticoids are tapered as ACTH recovers. Monitoring: morning cortisol, ACTH, and clinical assessment every 3-6 months initially. After bilateral adrenalectomy: immediate adrenal insufficiency requires lifelong glucocorticoid (hydrocortisone) and mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) replacement; monitoring at each endocrinology visit. After ectopic tumor resection: biochemical remission expected immediately; cortisol normalization within days.
complications: Cardiovascular disease — the leading cause of death in Cushing's syndrome; hypertension, dyslipidemia, insulin resistance, and central obesity collectively increase MI and stroke risk 2-5 fold; excess mortality is primarily cardiovascular

Osteoporosis and vertebral fractures — cortisol suppresses bone formation and calcium absorption; compression fractures occur in 20-40% of patients and may be silent; DEXA scan at diagnosis and bisphosphonate therapy as indicated
Diabetes mellitus — cortisol-induced insulin resistance causes type 2 diabetes in 20-40% of patients; often improves significantly after hypercortisolism is controlled but may persist

Infections — hypercortisolism causes immunosuppression (impairs neutrophil migration, macrophage function, and T-cell responses); opportunistic infections (Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, fungal infections) are a significant cause of morbidity and mortality
Psychiatric complications — depression, anxiety, cognitive impairment, and acute psychosis are common; may persist after biochemical remission; psychiatric follow-up is essential
Hypokalemia — in severe ectopic ACTH syndrome, cortisol acts as a mineralocorticoid causing profound potassium wasting; requires urgent correction with IV potassium and control of hypercortisolism

Adrenal insufficiency after treatment — successful treatment (tumor resection) removes the source of cortisol excess, causing transient or permanent adrenal insufficiency that requires glucocorticoid replacement

supportResources.name: The Cushing's Support and Research Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organization providing support, education, and research funding for Cushing's syndrome patients and families.

supportResources.name: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guidelines: Cushing's Syndrome. 2015.
supportResources.description: The foundational clinical practice guideline for Cushing's syndrome diagnosis and management.

supportResources.name: NICE NG24: Adrenal Insufficiency and Cushing's Syndrome. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2020.
supportResources.description: The UK clinical guideline covering both adrenal insufficiency and Cushing's syndrome management.

supportResources.name: Nieman LK, et al. Treatment of Cushing's Syndrome: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism. 2015;100(8):2807-2831.
supportResources.description: The Endocrine Society guideline on treatment of Cushing's syndrome.

confusedWithConditions.name: Simple Obesity
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Simple obesity does not cause moon facies, buffalo hump, proximal muscle weakness, purple striae, easy bruising, or hypertension. These features distinguish Cushing's from metabolic syndrome and simple obesity. A patient with Cushing's has a specific phenotype, not just weight gain.

confusedWithConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Severe depression causes hypercortisolism (pseudo-Cushing's) that can clinically mimic Cushing's. The distinction is made by testing — depression-related hypercortisolism normalizes when the depression is treated, while true Cushing's does not. Bipolar disorder, anxiety disorders, and other psychiatric conditions do not cause hypercortisolism.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PCOS causes hirsutism, acne, and irregular periods but does not cause moon facies, purple striae, proximal muscle weakness, hypertension, or hypokalemia. The distinctive physical features of Cushing's are absent in PCOS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary (Essential) Hypertension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary hypertension does not explain the constellation of features seen in Cushing's — central obesity, purple striae, proximal weakness, easy bruising, hypokalemia. A young person with new hypertension plus any Cushing's features should be screened.

relatedConditions.name: Addison's Disease (Adrenal Insufficiency)
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposite condition — insufficient cortisol production. After successful treatment of Cushing's syndrome, patients may have adrenal insufficiency requiring glucocorticoid replacement until the HPA axis recovers.

relatedConditions.name: Pheochromocytoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both can co-occur in MEN2 syndrome; pheochromocytoma must be ruled out before adrenal surgery in Cushing's syndrome to avoid hypertensive crisis.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary Adenoma
relatedConditions.relevance: ACTH-secreting pituitary adenomas are the cause of Cushing's disease. Non-functioning, GH-secreting, and prolactin-secreting pituitary adenomas are distinct but co-occur in MEN1.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Cushing's disease and Cushing's syndrome?
faqs.answer: Cushing's disease specifically refers to a pituitary adenoma secreting ACTH (Cushing's disease is the most common cause of endogenous Cushing's syndrome). Cushing's syndrome is the broader term — it refers to any state of excess cortisol, whether from pituitary ACTH, ectopic ACTH, adrenal tumors, or exogenous steroid use. In short: Cushing's disease is a type of Cushing's syndrome.

faqs.question: How is Cushing's syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis involves three steps: (1) Confirm excess cortisol using one of: late-night salivary cortisol, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, or dexamethasone suppression test. Two abnormal tests confirm hypercortisolism. (2) Measure ACTH — elevated ACTH means ACTH-dependent (pituitary or ectopic source); suppressed ACTH means ACTH-independent (adrenal tumor or steroid medications). (3) Find the source — for ACTH-dependent, pituitary MRI and high-dose dexamethasone suppression test; if inconclusive, bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling (BIPSS) is the gold standard.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Cushing's syndrome?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include: rapid weight gain with central (abdominal) obesity; rounded 'moon' face; fat pad at the back of the neck ('buffalo hump'); wide, purple striae on the abdomen and flanks; easy bruising and thin skin; proximal muscle weakness (difficulty climbing stairs or rising from a chair); new hypertension; new diabetes; irregular periods or loss of libido; hirsutism; acne; depression, anxiety, or cognitive changes; osteoporosis and fractures. Not all symptoms are present in every patient.

faqs.question: What causes Cushing's syndrome?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is prolonged use of corticosteroid medications (prednisone, dexamethasone) for autoimmune or inflammatory conditions — called iatrogenic Cushing's. The most common cause of endogenous Cushing's is a pituitary adenoma secreting ACTH (Cushing's disease, 70% of endogenous cases). Less common causes include ectopic ACTH-secreting tumors (small cell lung cancer, carcinoid tumors), adrenal adenomas, and adrenal carcinomas.

faqs.question: How is Cushing's syndrome treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. For Cushing's disease (pituitary adenoma), transsphenoidal surgery (TSS) to remove the tumor is first-line — it achieves remission in 65-90% of patients when performed by an experienced neurosurgeon. For adrenal tumors, surgical adrenalectomy is curative. For ectopic ACTH tumors, surgical removal of the tumor is curative when possible; if not, medications to block cortisol production (ketoconazole, metyrapone, mifepristone) or bilateral adrenalectomy are used. After treatment, patients require glucocorticoid replacement until their HPA axis recovers.

faqs.question: Is Cushing's syndrome curable?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most cases — surgical removal of the tumor (pituitary, adrenal, or ectopic) is often curative. After successful surgery, most Cushing's features improve or resolve over months to years, and life expectancy returns to normal. However, some features are irreversible — osteoporosis and vertebral fractures persist, skin changes improve but may not fully resolve. Recurrence occurs in 10-30% of Cushing's disease patients over 5-10 years, requiring ongoing monitoring.

faqs.question: Why does Cushing's cause hypertension and diabetes?
faqs.answer: Cortisol acts as a mineralocorticoid at high concentrations, causing sodium retention and potassium wasting, which raises blood pressure. Cortisol also increases glucose production by the liver, reduces glucose uptake in muscle and fat, and promotes visceral fat accumulation — together causing insulin resistance and diabetes (often called 'steroid diabetes'). Both hypertension and diabetes typically improve significantly once hypercortisolism is controlled.

faqs.question: What is Nelson syndrome?
faqs.answer: Nelson syndrome is a complication that can occur after bilateral adrenalectomy for Cushing's disease — the removal of the adrenal glands removes the negative feedback that cortisol provided to the pituitary tumor, allowing the residual pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma to grow rapidly. It causes very high ACTH levels, skin hyperpigmentation (from ACTH and MSH cross-reactivity), and in severe cases, compression of brain structures. Requires MRI surveillance post-adrenalectomy and treatment with radiotherapy or repeat surgery.

faqs.question: Can Cushing's syndrome come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes — recurrence of Cushing's disease (pituitary) occurs in approximately 10-30% of patients over 5-10 years after initial successful surgery. This requires ongoing monitoring — morning cortisol or 24-hour urinary free cortisol testing every 1-2 years. Recurrence can be treated with repeat transsphenoidal surgery, pituitary radiotherapy, bilateral adrenalectomy, or medical therapy.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after Cushing's treatment?
faqs.answer: After successful tumor removal, recovery is gradual. Hypertension and diabetes improve within weeks to months. Muscle weakness improves over 3-6 months. Mental health symptoms improve over 6-12 months. Bone density recovery takes years. The HPA axis — which was suppressed by excess cortisol — needs 6-18 months to recover, during which time patients require glucocorticoid replacement therapy. Some patients have permanent adrenal insufficiency and need lifelong replacement.

faqs.question: What medications are used to treat Cushing's syndrome?
faqs.answer: Medications used to control cortisol levels include: ketoconazole (blocks cortisol synthesis enzymes; most commonly used), metyrapone (blocks 11-beta-hydroxylase; rapidly reduces cortisol), mifepristone (blocks cortisol action at the receptor; helps with metabolic complications), pasireotide (somatostatin analog that reduces ACTH secretion; injected subcutaneously). These are used as preoperative preparation, for persistent disease after surgery, or when surgery is not possible.

faqs.question: Is Cushing's syndrome fatal if untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated Cushing's syndrome significantly increases mortality — primarily from cardiovascular disease (heart attack, stroke), infections (due to immunosuppression), and suicide. The excess mortality is substantial — untreated severe Cushing's carries a 5-year survival of approximately 50%. With appropriate treatment and biochemical remission, mortality normalizes toward that of the general population.

metaTitle: Cushing's Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Cushing's syndrome (hypercortisolism): symptoms, causes, diagnosis, and treatment. Expert-reviewed guide from endocrinologists.

images.altText: MRI image showing a pituitary adenoma in the sella turcica region
images.caption: A pituitary adenoma secreting ACTH causes Cushing's disease — the most common form of endogenous Cushing's syndrome.

images.altText: Bilateral adrenal glands showing hyperplasia in Cushing's syndrome
images.caption: In Cushing's disease, elevated ACTH drives bilateral adrenal hyperplasia, producing excess cortisol.

images.altText: Diagram showing the HPA axis — hypothalamus produces CRH, pituitary produces ACTH, adrenal glands produce cortisol
images.caption: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis: CRH stimulates ACTH, which stimulates cortisol production. In Cushing's disease, a pituitary tumor disrupts this feedback loop.
